dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for chest pain at a residence in Richmond.
Hi there, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What's bringing you to 911 today?
My chest has been hurting for the past 30 to 40 minutes. It feels sharp, like a stabbing under my left breast.
Can you tell me your full name?
Sylvia Jones.
Great. What county are we in right now?
Henrico County.
Which day is it today?
Probably Tuesday.
Could you tell me what happened today that got us here?
I started getting sharp chest pains. It might've been earlier.
Can we do a quick check of your breathing while we talk?
If you don't mind, let me just test your breath sounds.
Your breathing appears normal with a regular rate and depth. No signs of distress.
Your oxygen saturation is 96% while on 3 liters of oxygen. That's a good level.
Your respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute.
Your blood pressure is 129 over 87 mmHg. Pulse is 68.
Your blood sugar is at 113 mg/dL-everything seems stable.
I'll check your skin now. Overall appearance seems consistent with your general condition.
Could you describe exactly where the pain is?
It begins right under my left breast and radiates to my left shoulder. It comes and goes in waves.
Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Of course. Here we go.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain right now?
About 7. It sometimes hits 10 in waves, but it's better now.
Does the pain spread to other areas like the jaw or arm?
No, just the shoulder. No other areas.
Are you okay if we ask about your medical history? Some examples include heart disease or COPD?
Yes. I have a history of COPD, high blood pressure, heart failure, diabetes, and glaucoma.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take a lot. The nurse helps me track them. They include acetaminophen, Lasix, furosemide, metformin, atorvastatin, Albuterol, and Omeprazole.
Do you have any known allergies?
I told the nurse I have an allergy to aspirin. I also react to iodine or IV contrast dye.
The EKG initially showed a junctional rhythm with no major ST-segment changes. Let's take another look to be sure.
Given your allergy to aspirin, we won't give you ASA. That's okay with you?
We'd like to start an IV in your arm or leg. Which would be better?
Either is fine.
I have it. There may be some pressure and perhaps a slight pinch.
Let me check your pulse again-it's now 63 bpm. It's steady.
Your blood pressure is 143 over 92, and oxygen is now at 100%.
How's the IV feeling so far?
It doesn't hurt at all. It's fine.
Rechecking the EKG now-confirmed junctional rhythm, mild T wave inversion, and subtle anterior infarct pattern.
Your respiratory rate is now 17 breaths per minute. Still breathing steady.
Your blood sugar hasn't been measured further, but your initial reading was 113 mg/dL.
The EKG shows T wave abnormality and signs of a subtle anterior infarct, possible lateral and inferior ischemia, along with right ventricular hypertrophy.
We're following protocol 2-1, medical chest pain with suspected cardiac involvement.
Chippenham is the destination due to the cardiac concerns and your complex medical history of heart disease, COPD, and diabetes.
Would you like to assist in moving onto the cot, or do you prefer we help you?
I can move myself. I'd like to do it under my own power.
Got it. I'll stabilize the cot for you while you take your time.
How are your legs feeling? Can you take this step by step?
Yes, just give me a second.
Great. Moving to the cot will help with comfort during transport.
The cot is ready and locked. I'll help place you on it if you prefer or you can do it on your own.
I heard you were staying in the hospital last week. How did that go?
I was monitored for COPD and heart issues-everything was stable enough to get me out.
Good to hear. We've noted in your record that you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (A&Ox4).
I'll ensure your record reflects that choice, not as a refusal, but as a personal preference for mobility.
Sylvia, you're all set for transport. Ready when you are to move slowly as needed.
Let me double-check your pulse again. Still 63 bpm. Everything's steady.
We'll include in your records that you prefer to move under your own power, not as a refusal, just personal choice.
The transport vehicle is ready. We'll load you in slowly while you set the pace.
Great teamwork, Sylvia. You moved with A&O×4 awareness. Very helpful.
We're heading to Chippenham ED now. Traffic could pick up, but the ambulance is ready and you'll be safe.
Just yell if you need anything. We're with you for the ride.
Ahead notifications have gone out to the ER. They're aware of your symptoms, home nurse input, and vital progress.
Your oxygen is stable at 100%. Good job keeping it on again.
The ER will do a full 12-lead EKG. It might uncover more findings, especially on the posterior, but not yet in our lead.
How are you feeling with the IV and oxygen in place?
Sylvia, on a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your feeling now?
I'm about a 7. Not perfect, but better than earlier.
That's normal for the complexity, especially with COPD, heart, and diabetes history they manage daily.
Blood pressure is still at 143 over 92. Holding steady.
I'll start the transfer log now for hospital documentation.
Noting your prescriptions, allergies, and symptoms as part of the field data and EKG concerns.
I'll ensure the IV stays secure during transport.
It's a bit chilly in the ambulance. Just a heads-up in case you're sensitive.
Your pulmonary hypertension history will be noted for the ER for thorough monitoring.
You're still A&O×4. Great job through this, Sylvia.
Your GCS is 15 (Eye = 4, Verbal = 5, Motor = 6). Consistent throughout.
The T wave issues and possible right ventricular hypertrophy from the EKG will be highlighted for the ER team.
We've noted all current medications to avoid interactions. Particularly COPD and BP meds.
We calculated to show preference for movement under your control. It's noted as such in the chart.
Traffic light ahead, let's keep it slow until we pass that area.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how are you feeling now?
I'd say around 7. Not the best, but steady now.
That's good progress. You've been active in your care even in this situation.
Once at the hospital, the ER will do more extensive tests to sort out everything in full.
I'll keep you updated on our ETA when we get closer, Sylvia.
We've locked things in and are rolling toward the hospital.
Let's move slowly. I'll keep an eye on you all the way through.
Sylvia, you all set? Let's get you loaded now.
All set. On our way to the hospital with your condition monitored.
We're moving comfortably. Everything with you remains stable for the ride.
Chippenham is just around the corner. We'll make the correct city route to get there efficiently.
Pulse reading is 66. Holding stable.
Sylvia, quick double-check-IV is intact, no issues noted.
Final pulse reading before entering traffic lanes is at 66. Looks good.
We're moving comfortably. Everything with you remains stable for the ride.
Chippenham ER has been updated. They are aware of your vital signs and current status.
